question: Define a `Book` class with private members `title`, `author`, and `isbn` (string types). Implement a constructor to initialize these members and public getter methods for each. Include a `displayBookInfo` method that prints all book details.

test_cases.expected:
Book Details:
  Title: The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy
  Author: Douglas Adams
  ISBN: 978-0345391803

Getter Title: The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy
Getter Author: Douglas Adams
Getter ISBN: 978-0345391803

errors: The constructor takes `std::string` parameters by value (`std::string t, std::string a, std::string i`) and then uses assignment. This creates unnecessary copies: one for the parameter and another for the assignment to the member. Using `const std::string&` for parameters and an initializer list would be more efficient.

The `displayBookInfo` method uses `std::endl` on every line. `std::endl` not only inserts a newline character but also flushes the output buffer, which can be inefficient for frequent output operations. Using `\n` instead of `std::endl` for most lines and flushing only at the end (or when explicitly needed) would improve efficiency.
The constructor parameter names (`t`, `a`, `i`) are abbreviated, which reduces readability. Full, descriptive names like `title`, `author`, `isbn` would be clearer.

The `this->` pointer is used redundantly for accessing member variables within member functions (e.g., `this->title`). While technically correct, it adds visual clutter and is generally omitted when there's no ambiguity (e.g., when a local variable has the same name as a member).
